content:
2 min read
Just now

--

IBM took the unusual step of pre-announcing its earnings eight days ahead of its scheduled July 22, 2026 call, revealing a miss on both top and bottom lines. IBM has delivered one of its most disappointing quarters in recent years, not because of a collapse in demand but because enterprise technology spending shifted much faster than expected.
Preliminary Q2 revenue is expected to reach $17.2B (+1% YoY), below analyst expectations, while adjusted EPS of $2.93 also missed consensus. CEO Arvind Krishna was unusually candid, calling the quarter “disappointing” and attributing it to execution failures (large deals not closing on time), not just macro headwinds. The admission of execution failures on large-deal closures is itself a competitive signal. It suggests sales execution discipline, not just product-market fit. This is now a swing factor versus peers like Accenture (consulting) and Oracle/Microsoft (infrastructure & software bundling).
The market reacted aggressively, with IBM shares falling over 20%, indicating concerns that enterprise software spending may face prolonged pressure.
Top 5 Key Takeaways -

• Software remained resilient (+5%) but grew slower than investors expected.
o Red Hat remains the fastest-growing component
o Hybrid cloud platform adoption continues
o AI software portfolio remains healthy
o However, software growth slowed because enterprise CIOs temporarily prioritized hardware purchases over new application deployments.
2. Consulting was essentially flat, reflecting cautious enterprise spending. Reasons include:
o delayed digital transformation programs
o longer customer decision cycles
o enterprise budget scrutiny
o slower cloud migration projects
o AI consulting demand remains healthy but has not yet offset weakness in traditional consulting.
3. Infrastructure declined 7%, hurt by -
o weaker IBM Z demand
o delayed infrastructure upgrades
o customers prioritizing GPU clusters over traditional enterprise systems
4. Customers redirected budgets toward AI infrastructure (GPUs, servers, networking, memory) rather than software projects.
o The capex-before-opex shift (hardware over software/services ahead of anticipated price hikes) may be a broader signal of enterprises bracing for supply-constrained IT hardware markets, relevant to memory, storage, and server vendors industry-wide, not just IBM.
5. Strategic bet on Quantum Computing →$10B committed over five years across R&D, capex, manufacturing, M&A, and ecosystem-building, targeting a fault-tolerant quantum computer by 2029. Includes a letter of intent with the U.S. Department of Commerce to build Anderon, described as the first pure-play quantum wafer foundry, backed by $1B in CHIPS Act incentives plus $1B from IBM directly.
